replies.title: Be cautious with Ayahuasca
replies.content:
I was unable to find the original source, but I recall an American testimony on Ayahuasca experiences. The guy was familiar with psychedelics, and was introduced to the herb by a Southamerican national (albeit one of PMC background).

His recollection was that, during the trip, he came in contact with the spirits of Ayahuasca. There were several of them and they manifested as Amazonian indigenous humans. The eldest ones looked annoyed and just refused to deign acknowledge he was there, but one of the younger ones just kept mocking him and harassing him: "Why are you here?", "Go away!", "You don't know who you are!", "This was created not for you".

When this man tried to talk the young spirit, and when the spirit would not just laugh at him, he would ask her "What do you mean this was not created from me?", the response was: "you are not of the land". When he pushed for clarification, the spirit refused to use words and pushed images in his consciousness, of barefoot people living in the forest.

Just to clarify, I do not think the problem is that he is American. I am Mexican, and after hearing his recollections I would not touch Ayahuasca with a 10ft pole, even if psychedelics was my thing. I am too urban, too wealthy and too northern to fit that bill.

Now, that does not preclude you from having a better experience. But if you are serious about it, you should try to learn more about those people's culture; maybe travel and work there for a few months, create some kind of ties to the locals. It would also help to skip the more "Happy meal" kind of seminars and look for a more traditional teacher to get you initiated in that Sacrament.

replies.title: Re: Be cautious with Ayahuasca
replies.content:
This is all very interesting to hear! Thank you for sharing. Can definitely understand why certain spirits might be annoyed with you in cases like that.

Oddly, as it happens, I met some other people today who again invited me to take ayahuasca with them. I'd already decided to skip the previously one based off JMG's reply before I read this, but more food for thought.

For the record, the people I spoke to today are as white and Irish as I am, and they've had good experiences with it. Very strange stuff. Maybe one day I'll find out for myself, but probably not any time soon.

replies.title: Re: Be cautious with Ayahuasca

Banish afterwards and take a shower... If you can look for a Taita or equivalent, not someone posing like one as you risk your integrity --Taitas will take care of you considerably and not treat it like an a psychedelic orgy of sorts.

(I had thought I’d just go back to lurking quietly; but man plans, and God laughs, *so*...)

Anon, I’d be very respectfully curious to know where you got the teaching that, in Islam, no spiritual practice should ever be given up, even it it becomes burdensome. That’s very different from what my Master taught (and he gave thirteen centuries of examples, back to the Prophet himself). But, then, Islam is an Ocean.

As a for-instance: suppose as a young man one started keeping King David’s Fast (fasting every other day). And one kept it well into old age; but as an old man found that, like a lot of elderly people, he had difficulty staying at a healthy weight. In the systems I’m familiar with (I came to Islam as a Salafi, but now I’m an initiated Sufi), he would not only be permitted to stop his fast, but would be *required* to.

There is a sense, though, in which one has to keep up a spiritual practice life-long; and it fits pretty well with JMG’s teaching on having to keep up at least a minimal practice once one has started. To the Sufi, all Mankind is asleep. Once in a while, some of us start to wake just a little. And once someone has stirred, they might as well try to get fully awake, because they’ll never get back to sleep.

Another for instance: on my path, I woke up enough to see how many of the rulers of this world keep the populace under control through Sihr (evil magic). It wasn’t a particularly welcome thing to learn, and it’s made some things difficult. (I can no longer enjoy television, since TV- *all* TV, especially the commercials, but even PBS- looks like sihr to me.) And so I’ll be doing certain practices to keep myself and the people I care about from getting ensnared in the rulers’ sihr for the duration. (If this was what you originally meant, my apologies; we were just agreeing in different words.)

Many thanks to our gracious host for permitting these conversations in his comment section!

"I don't need demonic armor. I am strong enough with out it."

The affirmation is in the negative and needs improvement, but it worked by putting things in context and I was able to connect with people on a different level and accept the situation without getting disturbed. This seems like such a small thing but is actually a huge breakthrough. I am used to having to struggle with and suppress my problematic emotions to unpack later, but relaxing and accepting was comparatively effortless.


My question is about working with deities. So far with the 4 CGD deities I have only connected with Sirona more than superficially. Okay, the first thing to be aware of is that you can't legally claim a religious exemption to just one jab. Under the laws, it's all or nothing -- you aren't allowed to pick and choose. I'm not aware of another magical order that has a policy about vaccinations; the FRC are a bit of an outlier there, and the reason their policy is what it is is that it's been crafted very carefully to survive legal challenge.

Therefore maybe you should, no matter what divination says about it. replies.title: Re: Divination and "Interference"

comments.title: demons etc.
comments.content:
In the most recent COVID weekly post you host, you had mentioned Michael Hughes and people who are visited by demons in their childhood probably interacted with them in a previous incarnation.

In my childhood, I was frequently visited by two entities; they were quite unpleasant and enjoyed tormenting me. In my early teens, I was visited by a different entity, who taught me how to block the other two. It took a while for me to master the blocking and, if I was having a difficult time keeping the bad ones away, he would come and chase them off. When I got to the point where I could keep the bad ones away on my own, he gave me a name I could use to call him if I needed him. (To simplify the story, we'll say his name is Bob.)

I thought Bob was my guardian angel (of sorts... I saw him as a gargoyle, actually). I only called on him if I felt unsafe (like having to stay in a hotel on a business trip), because I figured he had other things to attend to and I didn't want to be greedy. Since my initial training, he has never visited me unless I called on him. When I got older, I would give him offerings of honey as a thank-you for protecting me when I was younger and teaching me to defend myself.

When working through CosDoc and reading your older posts, I have tried to determine what type of entity Bob is. I don't want him to be something unpleasant, and he has never ever done anything malevolent. All he has done is protect me and to teach me to protect myself. In one of your not-to-distant responses to someone, you had responded to another query about someone being contacted by their 'higher self'. I would like for Bob to be my higher self, and when I asked him if he was, I got a full-body glowy tingly feeling. (Communication between me and Bob is me asking questions and getting either affirmation or negation.)

But then when you discussed the COVID demon, you said that demons usually do one thing (such as seek consent). Bob has basically done one thing (protection). So now I'm back to wondering what Bob is.

Normally, I would ask for resources to read to help me identify what Bob is. But I really don't want to read up on demons. (I don't want to do anything that might make them notice me.) Based on what I've described, do you think Bob is benevolent? Maybe my higher self? Or should I be worried that he is a sneaky one with a long-term plan of lowering my resistance by being nice (for 40+ years, which I guess to these entities is really not that long).

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: demons etc.
replies.content:
Judge spirits by their behavior. If "Bob" has always been helpful and benevolent to you, whatever exactly he is, you can assume that he's a beneficent spirit. Whether or not he's your higher self, he's clearly there to help you, and I'd encourage you to cultivate the relationship.

How does "Bob" react to the names and symbols of deities?

replies.title: Re: demons etc.
replies.content:
I am rather new with deities. Monotheism never appealed to me. At first, I thought I should use a pantheon that had something to do with my heritage. My mother is Germanic and I have a set of runes that I use that work well for me, so I tried Norse. They were polite, but they didn't really want me. My tarot deck (that I've had for 35+ years) is based on Greek mythology. There is no Greek in my family history, but somewhere along the way I read a comment you made to the effect of 'you don't know what was in your history in past incarnations', so I tried Greek. The response was "Gee, it took you long enough." :-)

I got the most enthusiastic responses from Hermes, Apollo, and Artemis, so those are my focus, so far. But I have identified a pair (god and goddess) for each of the festival days and, after the preceding festival, ask the next two if they are agreeable to being honored at the next festival. After I get an affirmative, I study up on them and start finding things to put on my altar for them.

Pretty much every evening, I tell my deities either what I studied or learned that day, or if I noticed anything interesting, or if a tree or bird gifted me something, or if I got a new insight, or, if I had a dream, what I figured out. Sometimes as I'm describing something, I'll get a little tingle, so I know I did something right. I thank them individually and then tell Bob that I appreciate him and thank him. I *always* get a tingle from him.

All that to say, he doesn't seem to mind being around my deities or being included in the conversation.

He does have his own altar, though. Ages ago, I bought a gargoyle statue that screamed "BUY ME". It has lived on my mantle above my fireplace for decades and when I give him an offering, that's where I put it.

(PS: Thank you so much!!!)

replies.title: Re: demons etc.

Hello JMG, a few questions:

1. I noticed that LRP uses different god names for the elemental pentagrams than the GRP. In GRP it is YHVH-ALHIM-EL-ADNI, which corresponds to the elements. In LRP it is YHVH, ADNI, AHIH and AGLA. Of course GRP also uses AHIH and AGLA for the "spirit" pentagrams. The question is, why is that so? How does it change the meaning and purpose of the ritual? At least give me a hint.

2. What would be the purpose of a lesser ritual of hexagram in invoking mode? In banishing mode it prepares space for invocation of specific planetary or a sephirah energy using the GRH.

3. There is a fifth form of the hexagram in the LRH-two triangles pointing down. It was mentioned once by Israel Regardie, who said that it must be "employed with the very greatest care and in cases of communication with an evil force working thereunder". I found no further information on it. Could you elaborate, perhaps?

4. I would like to construct a ritual of the Septagram to invoke planetary energies, to circumvent the clumsy way of invoking the Sun with the GRH in the Golden Dawn tradition. Following the GRH formula, it should go like this:

0) Perform the Cabalistic Cross (or LVX signs silently if done after a ritual that ends with CC)
1) Draw an upward pointing septagram (like the one on this page) from the point that corresponds to the planet (clockwise to invoke). Draw it in the corresponding color and vibrate the corresponding god-name while drawing.
2) Point to the center of the septagram and vibrate ARARITA.
3) Trace a white line from the center of the sepatagram as you move in a clockwise direction around the edge of your circle’s space to the next direction (east-south-west-north). Repeat steps 1&2.
4) Once all 4 septagrams are drawn and charged, perform the Analysis of Keyword.
-End of ritual-

Does that sound about right to you, JMG?

5. Not a question, but I wrote a book review, it's on Damien Echols's book "Angels and Archangels". For those interested, you can read it here.

replies.title: Re: Questions on rituals
replies.content:
1) The four names used in the LBRP are not related to the elements or directions in any way. I'm pretty sure they could have used any set of four four-lettered names -- certainly that's been my experience in constructing LBRPs with other pantheons. Now that it's established, of course, using the existing set will be more effective because the egregor of the ritual will help you out.

2) There basically isn't one, which is why nobody uses it.

3) Nope. When you've gotten to the point where you can understand it through your own meditations, you'll know enough to use it intelligently.

4) The Aurum Solis uses something like that. Give it a try and see what results you get.

comments.title: Disclosure
comments.content:
Obviously this is a bit of a nuanced question but I wondered if opening yourself up too much to strangers - a la what is encouraged on social media and lots of business advice etc - actually punctures your aura ( I am struggling for the exact words ). It seems like many people are prompted to share the essence of themselves more and more - lots of details perhaps about finances or sex lives - then they are struck with real burn out or emotional dissolution. I was on a course where a talented teacher was coached on selling her course through writing a book about her troubled childhood.

I don't think it was a massive success, it was moving. It was a personal coach that told her it was essential to appeal through vulnerabilities and it feels like a mistake based on a cultural moment.

I have a ton of other examples but really I just want to know what are the energetics for this - or a view on them - is this opening yourself to unneccessary risk and is it wise to avoid ( I would anyway ). I am kind of wondering because of what you have shared about keeping things private.

replies.title: Re: Disclosure
replies.content: It's certainly not healthy. It's one thing to share such things with people you trust and care about, and quite another to splash around your vulnerabilities in public. Whether it actually involves a weakening of the aura would have to be settled on a case by case basis by competent clairvoyants.

replies.title: Re: Disclosure
replies.content: Ok thank you, it definitely annoyed me as advice and it often does seem to blow up emotionally 🙃 also I think it can detract from the dignity of skills acquired as all the focus is brought to the weak spots even if it is a triumph to overcome them and they are connective or humanising one to one. It took me away from several online spaces at no great loss tbh

replies.title: Re: Disclosure
replies.content: One other issue is that by exposing your vulnerabilities like this, even if it doesn't directly weaken your aura, would give those who want to weaken you extra avenues for attack...

comments.title: Kid struggling with nightmares and seeing things
comments.content:
My 8-year-old son has been struggling with nightmares for some years. They're not very frequent, but pretty damn scary when they occur, often involving monstrous beings, extreme violence and death. He's often lucid enough during these dreams that he knows it's a nightmare and sometimes tells me he had success in turning the dream around by summoning helpful beings to scare off the monsters, or even getting out of the dream by killing himself. (As a parent, that sounds brutal, as much as I'm glad that he has found a coping mechanism.)

During the last couple of years, he has grown increasingly scared of darkness, and when I ask him why, he tells me he's scared of things he "sees" in the dark. He won't provide much in the way of details, but apparently the "things" are related to what he encounters in the nightmares, and they scare the heck out of him.

I try to be supportive and acknowledge his subjective experiences, while assuring him that whatever these "things" are, they can't actually hurt him. But I can't bring myself to parrot a mechanistic explanation like "don't worry, it's just your brain making up things", as I myself feel quite strongly that the world of dreams and imagination has a kind of reality to it that shouldn't be casually dismissed.

Do you (JMG or others reading) have any advice on how to cope with this? I realize this isn't strictly a question about magic, but I don't really know where else to ask for advice, and I feel like there might be some touching points. (In case it's relevant, I'm familiar with magical techniques, but not really proficient at anything beyond simple natural magic.)

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Kid struggling with nightmares and seeing things
replies.content:
I was such a child as your son back in the 1940s, and my nightmares, like his, were too ghastly to talk much about once I woke up. Eventually my mother taught me a way to get rid of the nightmares, largely so she herself could finally get a good night's sleep without being awakened pretty much every night by a screaming, terrified child. Here's what she told me to do:

Pay attention at the moment when you're in your bed and just about to fall asleep. If you look around inside your mind, you will find a picture that is the start of the first dream you're going to have. It may look like it's going to be a dream that will scare you once it starts. If that's what it looks like, see the picture as a chalk drawing on a magic blackboard. There will be a magic eraser there, too. Pick up the eraser and erase the picture. Then your first dream will be about something else. It'll take you a little while to learn how to do this well, but you can do it--anyone can do it.

Once you've got that down and can make it work, the other thing you can do with that magic blackboard is to take the magic chalk that is there, too, and draw the first picture for a dream you'd enjoy having. With practice, that will let you shape your dreams any way you like.

It took me about eight months to get really good at this, but I did, and nightmares have hardly ever troubled me ever since then.

I'd bet your son could make this work for him with some months of practice.

One more thing (which my mother did not tell me, but I eventually figured out for myself as a 'teen). When some sort of other-than-human beings actually show up at night (and they're more than just your imagination playing tricks on you), the only way they have to take control of you is through fear. If you can manage not to fear them, no matter how hard they try to frighten you, then they can't do a thing with you (or to you). Once they've seen that you're too "strong" for any fear they can try to make you feel, they'll give up on you and go find other, easier victims.

And the same skills that you develop to control your dreams and destroy your nightmares will also work against each and every fear that such other-than-human beings try to make you feel.

replies.title: Re: Kid struggling with nightmares and seeing things
replies.content:
I had similar night terrors as a child. I was able to shake them off with a combination of learning to become lucid and wake myself, and IRL martial arts training that made me less helpless. Once I had methods of dealing with the dreams they stopped happening.

I question saying that it can't hurt you, it's not like Freddy Kruger is going to reach up from behind the pillow, but intense fear is not exactly healthy, physically or psychologically.

Seeing things in the dark is a side effect and comes from the misinterpretation of shadows, so a rather bright night-light would probably help.

The silver lining of all this is that it self-taught me how to lucid dream. It's hard to say that waking up screaming is worth it though.

comments.content:
Hello! Hope you and everyone here is doing well!

I asked you about a month or so ago about masculine/feminine etheric bodies. Thanks again for your writing on that! I've been mulling it over, and just want to make sure I'm understanding something correctly.

If I understand you right, all genders need to take in etheric energy to sustain themselves. The same way we need to take in air/water/food. Is that right? But feminine etheric bodies only take in energy, whereas masculine etheric bodies need to take in energy and then transmute it and pass it on. Or do masculine etheric bodies produce their own etheric energy inherently and then radiate it, and feminine bodies take it in? Basically, I guess the crux of it is I'm not clear if masculine etheric bodies also need to take in etheric energy from the world, or if they produce all they need themselves? Thanks!

***

On an unrelated note, I ran into a new concept recently, and I'm curious what you and the commentariat think.

I heard someone discussing medical diagnoses as "medical hexing", in the context of, for example, if you get a cancer diagnosis, it's going to be so upsetting that your body's responses will be depressed, and it could become a self-fulfilling prophecy. You expect to become sick, and thus you do. Or, in the context of mental illnesses, expecting that you have certain limitations, and thus you do.

I'm sure in reality it's a lot more complex than that, but if nothing else, I'm sure there's some variation of the placebo effect going on. Mostly, I'm just curious what your thoughts are.

***

Thanks as always for all the work you do. I really enjoy your writings.

replies.title: (no subject)

The masculine etheric body brings down energy from higher planes, transmutes it into etheric forms, and lets it flow outward; the feminine etheric body gathers in etheric energy from outside itself, transmutes it into higher forms, and lets it flow back up the planes.

2) It's called the nocebo effect -- "nocebo" is the opposite of "placebo." Yes, you can be harmed very effectively by a medical diagnosis.

He was definitely a spy. Back before espionage, like everything else, got co-opted by huge incompetent bureaucracies, there were plenty of freelance spies, some working for a single government, some available for hire. replies.title: Re: Illness and Ritual of the Rose Cross
replies.content:
1) Don't try to use magic in an urgent situation when you haven't learned and practiced it already! That would be like trying to drive your son to the hospital when you haven't yet learned how to drive. Give your son a lot of hugs and caring, and once he's on his way to a full recovery, then you can start learning magic so that you can do what you need to do if something else happens in the future.

2) You have a lot of work ahead of you. You are what in the old days used to be called a witch -- that is, someone with a natural talent for using magic, which usually functions negatively until and unless it's trained. The first thing you need to do is accept that. The second is to consider taking up a religious practice, so that you can have the help of deity in getting this power of yours under conscious control. The third is to consider serious magical training. I don't recommend Circles of Power for your first book; if you like Golden Dawn style magic, Learning Ritual Magic is the one to begin with, and once you've finished it you can go to Circles.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG and thank you again for these excellent and informative Magic Mondays!

Further to the discussion of "Peter Kelder's" Five Rites above, I was wondering:

1. How many times should each rite be repeated for a reasonably effective benefit? I currently do each of them 10-15 times in a row, except for the spinning, which gives me nausea if I repeat it more than once.
2. When repeating, should I be doing the repeated ones in a set, e.g. ten "leg lifts", then ten "back bends", etc. or repeating as a group of all 5, once through, then another set of all 5, like a sun salutation?
3. I assume you put "Peter Kelder" in quotes for a reason. Obviously you're saying it's a nom de plume of some sort - but should we be able to recognize or figure out who it's the nom de plume of? Is it an anagram of someone? Is it useful/beneficial to identify this?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Start by doing the Rites three times each once a day. Add an additional repetition each week until you're up to 21 repetitions. If you're getting nausea from the spinning in #1, slow down -- seriously, slow way down -- until you can do three reps without feeling queasy. (If you have trouble even moving slowly, do what ballet dancers do -- fix your gaze on a point on the wall and keep it there while you turn.) Moving fast isn't necessary, or even helpful.

2) Each rite is done as a set. You might want to download a copy of the original book, which you can get here among other places online; it covers a lot of details you won't find elsewhere.

3) "Peter Kelder" is known to be a pseudonym. There's been a lot of speculation about who hid behind it, but I'm pretty sure it was written by Augusta Foss Heindel or someone very closely associated with her in the Rosicrucian Fellowship in California in the 1930s. A lot of what's in "Kelder's" book has obvious connections with RF teachings, and thus with those of other groups in that end of the Rosicrucian movement.

replies.title: Re: Sense of the Machine

Buy cheap old second-hand machines from the pre-plastics era (i.e. all metal) at your local junk shop or antiques mall. Take them apart and put them back together, maybe several times if they are at all complex. (Buit start with simple ones first, and work your way up to music boxes or ship's clocks or combination locks.) The machines themselves will teach you more than books can. Although I ended up a medieval philologist, it was due to happenstance and/or calling; originally I had planned to become a locksmith, and spent many many hours as a 'teen taking locks apart, studying how they worked, figuring out how to pick them, and reading almost all the available books on the design and construction of locks.

"All was a Vision, all a Dream: the Furnaces became

Of Heaven Fourfold among the Visions of God in Eternity

comments.title: Did I do that?
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
I’d like to ask you about the act of manifesting physical objects. I’ve had two occurrences in my life where circumstances required an item that was not available to me and that I was unable to find in the expected places. I proceeded to basically obsess and fume about not being able to obtain the item, and then, a short time later, when I had basically released my agitation and frustration and obsession and “moved on” so to speak, Bam! There’s the thing that I was looking for, right in front of me, lying unlooked for in the most improbable and unexpected location. The first time this happened, I was trying to mail a package that I really wanted to get out that day. I needed packing tape. We were out. I went to three stores trying to buy some and came up empty. I became more and more frustrated and exasperated as the search went on, and remember being a bit surprised at just how I intensively I felt that I was obsessing about getting that roll of packing tape. Finally, after my third unsuccessful stop to buy tape, I gave up and let it go. I decided to go to a city park and garden instead. It was a rainy winter day in Portland and nobody was in the parking lot or at the entry gate to the garden. It was utterly deserted. And there, lying on the walkway right in the middle of the entry way, was a roll of packing tape. It gave me goosebumps and quite a sense of excitement. I quietly thanked whoever left the tape there for me to find. I’ll spare the details of the second instance in order to save a bit your valuable time, but basically I was en route to a local beach, didn’t have any sunscreen, came up empty handed in 3 different stores, mentally obsessed and fumed about how I was going to fry every part of my body in the summer sun, and when I get to the completely deserted beach, there on a dune in front of me, without a soul in sight, is a full bottle of sunscreen. I don’t feel that these were coincidences. I think I may have somehow put so much energy and focus onto one specific thing that it somehow made it appear in my life at that time. I’m not magically trained. I’ve started the DMH and set it aside without even finishing the full SOP. So I have no skill but this was something. Can you give me any insight as to what was going on here? Is this a talent that I could develop? Thank you very much for your time and this forum.

replies.title: Re: Did I do that?
replies.content: That's really quite remarkable. If you did do it, that's an unusual skill, and not one I've ever seen discussed in the magical literature; the term for it is apportation, and you might be able to find something under that heading.

I’d like to work out a few experiments, although I have a feeling that the emotional component may have provided a lot of the fuel, so to speak, and I don’t know how I could replicate the conditions willingly.
